lessons.title: Mastering the useEffect Lifecycle & Cleanup
lessons.notes:
### Key Takeaways from useEffect Lifecycle
- **Cleanup function**: Executes before the hook runs again and upon unmounting. Crucial for resetting event listeners and clearing WebSockets.
- **Dependency array**: React uses `Object.is` for comparison. Avoid arrays or objects directly in dependency arrays without stable memoization (`useMemo` / `useCallback`).
- **Race conditions**: Always use a clean-up flag `active = true` inside the fetch callback, setting it to `false` in cleanup to disregard delayed network responses.
objectives: Identify why the cleanup callback triggers
Avert memory leaks and subscription overlaps
Handle async API calls safely inside hooks

resources.title: A Complete Guide to useEffect by Dan Abramov

quiz.question: When does the cleanup function in useEffect run?
options: Only when the component is unmounted.
Before every re-run of the effect and when the component is unmounted.
Directly after the main effect function finishes execution.
When state variables inside the component change, regardless of dependencies.
quiz.explanation: The cleanup function runs immediately before running the effect again with new dependencies, as well as when the component unmounts. This guarantees zero leakage.

quiz.question: Including a raw javascript object directly in the dependency array can cause infinite rendering loops.
quiz.explanation: True. Because React uses reference equality (Object.is) for dependencies, a new object reference created on every render triggers the effect again.

assignments.title: Secure Socket Cleanup Handler
assignments.requirements: Build a custom hook `useWebSocket` that connects to a URL, exposes messages, and properly calls disconnect on unmount.
assignments.feedback: Perfect socket disposal block inside the cleanup function. Robust and lightweight.

lessons.title: Optimizing Computations with useMemo & useCallback
lessons.notes:
### useMemo vs useCallback
- `useMemo`: Caches the **result** of a function computation.
- `useCallback`: Caches the **function instance** itself.
- Useful for avoiding expensive calculations on every render cycle.
- **Rule of Thumb**: Do not over-optimize. Only use when there are measurable render bottlenecks or when passing callbacks to memoized children using `React.memo`.
objectives: Distinguish memoized values from cached handler functions
Identify when memoization is actually necessary
Optimize rendering of list items and callbacks

quiz.question: What is the primary difference between useMemo and useCallback?
options: useMemo returns a memoized value; useCallback returns a memoized function.
useMemo runs asynchronously; useCallback runs synchronously.
useMemo is deprecated in React 19; useCallback is the modern replacement.
useMemo only supports array dependencies; useCallback supports objects.
quiz.explanation: useMemo stores the computation output, whereas useCallback returns the function itself to avoid trigger duplication in child trees.

lessons.title: Managing Complex State with useReducer
lessons.notes:
### useReducer Overview
`useReducer` is an alternative to `useState` for complex state logic.

It takes a reducer function of type `(state, action) => newState` and an initial state, returning the current state paired with a `dispatch` method.
objectives: Decouple state transitions from rendering templates

modules.title: Next.js App Router & Server Components
modules.description: Shift your mental model from client-rendered to hybrid server/client applications utilizing modern routing frameworks.

lessons.title: RSC vs Client Components: The Core Difference
lessons.notes:
### React Server Components (RSC) Summary
- **Server-Side only execution**: RSCs compile and execute on the server, generating static JSX payloads. Zero impact on browser bundle sizes!
- **Data Fetching**: Directly await promises/databases in RSC async functions.
- **Boundaries**: Client components are marked with `'use client'`. Server components can import client components, but client components cannot import server components directly (must pass them as `children` or props).

lessons.title: Streaming and Suspense Boundaries
lessons.notes:
### Streaming with Suspense
Streaming allows you to break down the page's HTML into smaller chunks and progressively stream those chunks from the server to the client.

lessons.title: HTTP/3 Deep Dive & QUIC Protocol Analysis
lessons.notes:
### QUIC Protocol Overview
QUIC replaces TCP with UDP on the transport layer to provide stream multiplexing and connection migration.

lessons.title: Introduction to gRPC and Protocol Buffers
lessons.notes:
### gRPC & Protocol Buffers Summary
- **HTTP/2 Transport**: Leverages bidirectional streaming and header compression.
- **Protocol Buffers**: Strongly typed binary serialization instead of bulky JSON, resulting in significant network efficiency.
- **Usage**: Perfect for internal microservice communication where throughput is paramount.

modules.title: Arrays & Sliding Window

lessons.title: LeetCode 1: Two Sum (Easy)
lessons.notes:
### Two Sum Analysis
- **Brute Force**: $O(n^2)$ time complexity using double loops.
- **Hash Map Solution**: $O(n)$ time and space complexity.
- **Logic**: Iterate and record difference `target - current_value` in hash map. If value exists in hash map, indices are located.

lessons.title: LeetCode 125: Valid Palindrome (Easy)
lessons.notes:
### Valid Palindrome Notes
- Use two pointers, one at start ($L = 0$) and one at end ($R = len - 1$).
- Move pointers inward, skipping non-alphanumeric characters using `isalnum()`.
- Compare case-insensitive characters. Return `false` immediately on mismatch. If indices cross, return `true`.
